model_answer: Key risks include: (1) Non-identifiability—if measurements are too sparse, lack temporal variation, or approach steady state, alpha cannot be uniquely determined. (2) Missing boundary/initial conditions—these cause non-uniqueness and produce unphysical reconstructions near the domain edges and initial time regardless of interior fit. (3) Noise overfitting—unknown noise statistics can bias alpha and corrupt the temperature field if the data loss is over-weighted. (4) Optimization failure—poor collocation coverage or imbalanced losses can trap the network in local minima with high PDE residuals. (5) Model misspecification—if alpha is spatially varying but treated as constant, residual errors will persist and temperature predictions will be systematically biased. (6) Extrapolation—reconstruction quality degrades outside the spatial and temporal support of the sensors.

(2) Missing IC/BCs cause ill-posedness; validation error on held-out sensors may be low for wrong nu if the network compensates with incorrect boundary behavior. (3) Noise bias: noisy second-derivative estimates can skew nu; mitigate with robust losses or data smoothing. (4) Small-nu stiffness: standard MLPs may underresolve sharp gradients and converge to an inflated nu; monitor spectral content and increase collocation density near layers. (5) Hyperparameter sensitivity: static loss weights often cause training failure; validate via residual convergence and parameter sensitivity studies. (6) Domain coverage gaps: poor generalization outside the convex hull of data and collocation points.

Use a single fully connected neural network with two output heads predicting u and f, or coupled networks, with sinusoidal/Fourier feature embeddings to mitigate spectral bias and capture potentially high-frequency source structures. model_answer: Choose between a single-instance solver and a reusable operator surrogate based on operational context. (1) Single-instance: Use when only one K-field is studied, no labeled data exists, and guaranteed accuracy for that specific field is preferred over fast inference. Recommend a mixed Multiscale Finite Element Method (MsFEM) or a domain-decomposed PINN (XPINN) with multiscale Fourier features, because pure global PINNs suffer spectral bias against fine channels. (2) Reusable surrogate: Use when many K-realizations are queried repeatedly, a distribution p(K) is available, and low latency is required. Recommend a Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) or Physics-Informed Neural Operator (PINO) trained on high-fidelity (K,h) pairs. FNO captures long-range channel correlations in spectral space. If training data is limited, use a DeepONet with physics-informed regularization. The deciding factors are: query volume, cost of offline data generation, availability of a K-distribution, and the trade-off between generalization error (surrogate) and per-case computational cost (single-instance).

Mitigate with Fourier feature embeddings, adaptive collocation refinement, and hp-VPINN variants. (2) Temporal error accumulation: the nonlinear convection term u·∂u/∂x couples errors across time. Causal training and time-marching (solving sequentially over shorter time windows) reduce this risk. (3) Periodic BC violation if architectural enforcement is not used: soft penalty enforcement can fail for steep gradients near boundaries. The proposed periodic feature encoding avoids this entirely. (4) Training stagnation: the balance between L_PDE and L_IC is sensitive; if the IC loss is not sufficiently small before PDE training, the solution may drift. Monitor both losses separately and adjust λ weights dynamically. (5) Lack of validation data: if no observational or reference solution data is available, validation relies solely on PDE residual magnitude and IC satisfaction, which may not detect physically incorrect solutions that satisfy the PDE weakly. Recommend generating a reference solution via spectral or finite-difference methods for comparison if computational resources allow.

(4) Poor α convergence: flat loss landscapes near the true α. Mitigation: multi-start optimization; verify α stabilizes across runs. (5) Collocation point insufficiency: too few or poorly distributed points yield weak PDE enforcement. Mitigation: increase N_c and verify residual decreases uniformly. (6) Stiff dynamics: if α is large, sharp temporal gradients may require adaptive collocation or causal training to respect time causality.

model_answer: (1) Non-identifiability: if data is too sparse or confined to a small region, multiple (ν, u) pairs may satisfy both the PDE and the data equally well. Mitigation: explicitly flag this as a blocking issue; if approximate BCs/ICs are assumed, quantify sensitivity of ν̂ to these assumptions. (3) Noise amplification: noisy gradients from the data loss can destabilize training and bias ν̂. Mitigation: use robust loss functions (e.g., Huber loss), increase PDE weighting, and employ early stopping based on a held-out validation subset. (4) Shock formation at low ν: strong-form PDE residual is ill-defined at discontinuities, causing training failure. Mitigation: if ν is suspected to be very small, switch to a weak-form or variational PINN formulation. (5) Local minima in the joint (θ, ν) optimization landscape. Mitigation: multi-start training, learning rate scheduling, and monitoring the PDE residual on a dense validation grid. (6) Overfitting to noise: the network may fit noisy observations at the expense of PDE consistency. Mitigation: track both L_data and L_pde on validation data; L_pde should remain low.

Validation metrics: relative L² error on held-out u observations, PDE residual norm, and if a ground-truth f is available for benchmarking, relative L² error on f.

Important caveat: PINNs are known to struggle with hyperbolic PDEs due to propagating discontinuities and multi-scale temporal dynamics. Mitigations include causality-weighted loss (temporal weighting that prioritizes earlier times), characteristic-aligned sampling, and Fourier feature embeddings for better high-frequency capture.

model_answer: (1) Non-uniqueness: with incomplete BCs/ICs, multiple wave fields may satisfy both the PDE and sparse data. Without at least one fully specified boundary condition and partial initial condition information, the problem is under-determined—reflected waves and traveling wave directions become ambiguous. (2) Hyperbolic PDE difficulty: PINNs often fail to capture sharp wavefronts and interference patterns; the network may produce smooth, low-frequency solutions that fit data but violate true dynamics. (3) Insufficient sensor coverage: if sensors are too sparse relative to the wave speed and domain size, the data cannot constrain the solution between sensors, leading to large uncertainty. (4) Boundary condition misidentification: assuming the wrong BC type (e.g., Dirichlet vs. Neumann) introduces systematic error that cannot be corrected by training. (5) Overfitting to noise: with sparse data, the network may fit measurement noise while the PDE loss remains insufficiently weighted. (6) Temporal causality violation: without causality-aware training, the network may satisfy the PDE locally but produce acausal solutions. Mitigations: report uncertainty via ensemble training or Bayesian PINN; require the team to provide at minimum one BC and the BC type at each boundary; perform identifiability analysis before full training; use held-out sensor data (if available) for validation.

Justification: (1) PINNs naturally embed known PDE structure as soft constraints in the loss, which is critical when data is sparse and noisy—the physics regularizes the fit; (2) the coupled reaction terms R₁,R₂ are known and differentiable, enabling automatic-differentiation-based residual computation; (3) sparse point data is the regime where PINNs outperform data-driven neural operators; (4) two output heads share spatial-temporal feature extraction while allowing separate field representations. Architecture: modified MLP with Fourier feature embeddings for better high-frequency capture, skip connections for gradient flow stability, and tanh/sin activations for smoothness. If D₁,D₂ are unknown, they are parameterized as learnable scalars with log-transformation to enforce positivity.

(3) Missing boundary/initial conditions: incorrect assumptions propagate as systematic bias throughout the domain; no validation signal exists at boundaries if unobserved. (4) Reaction term stiffness: if R₁,R₂ involve fast-slow dynamics (e.g., Michaelis-Menten, activator-inhibitor), PDE residuals may have large gradients causing training instability—adaptive collocation or causal training may be needed. (5) Validation metrics: standard holdout MSE is unreliable with sparse data; use PDE residual norms on dense collocation grids, conservation law checks, and physical plausibility (non-negativity, boundedness) as supplementary metrics. (6) Mode collapse between u and v: the coupling may allow the network to swap field identities if initial conditions are unconstrained—must enforce distinct initial condition parameterization.

The Neumann BC is a derivative-level constraint and is typically harder to enforce accurately than the Dirichlet BC, requiring careful weighting.

Architecture: inputs (x, t) → output u(x,t), with 4–6 hidden layers of 50–100 neurons each and Tanh activations (smooth, compatible with second-order derivatives via automatic differentiation). Justification: (1) the 1D heat equation is a linear parabolic PDE with smooth solutions, well-suited for PINNs; (2) mixed Dirichlet-Neumann BCs are naturally handled through separate loss terms without requiring mesh-dependent discretization; (3) automatic differentiation cleanly computes ∂u/∂x for the Neumann flux constraint; (4) no scattered observation data is mentioned, so a purely physics-driven PINN is appropriate. Alternative: if α is unknown and observation data were available, the network could be extended to output (u, α) for inverse problems. For very long time horizons, a time-marching PINN or causal training variant should be considered to mitigate temporal causality issues.

model_answer: Three essential physics constraints must be enforced: (1) Mass conservation: d/dt ∫_Ω φ dΩ = 0, which holds under no-flux boundary conditions and is the primary conserved quantity; any drift indicates model failure. (2) Free energy dissipation: dF/dt = −∫_Ω M(φ)|∇μ|² dΩ ≤ 0, guaranteeing thermodynamic consistency and monotonic energy decay. (3) The constitutive relation μ = δF/δφ = f'(φ) − κ∇²φ linking chemical potential to the variational derivative of the free energy. Boundary conditions must be specified to close the system; no-flux (∇μ·n = 0) and neutral wetting (∇φ·n = 0) are standard but must be confirmed. The double-well structure of f ensures phase separation with equilibrium at φ = ±1.

model_answer: Use a mixed-formulation PINN splitting the fourth-order equation into two coupled second-order equations: (1) μ = f'(φ) − κ∇²φ and (2) ∂φ/∂t = ∇·(M(φ)∇μ). This avoids computing fourth-order derivatives, improves numerical stability, and naturally provides μ as a network output for loss computation. Employ two separate neural networks (or a multi-output network) for φ and μ. Use a modified MLP architecture (e.g., with Fourier feature embeddings or sinusoidal activations) to capture sharp interface gradients. Justification: the mixed formulation is standard for Cahn-Hilliard numerics, reduces derivative order from four to two (critical for automatic differentiation accuracy), and allows separate enforcement of the chemical potential relation. The architecture must handle the multiscale nature: diffuse interfaces of width O(√κ) within a larger domain.

model_answer: Primary risks: (1) Mass conservation drift — the most likely and consequential failure mode; pointwise PDE residuals do not guarantee integral conservation. Mitigate with explicit integral constraints and monitor ∫φ dΩ at every evaluation epoch. (2) Sharp interface under-resolution — insufficient collocation points near interfaces leads to smeared or oscillatory profiles. Mitigate with adaptive sampling and interface-tracking heuristics. (3) Training instability from stiff dynamics — the Cahn-Hilliard equation has vastly different timescales (fast coarsening vs. slow late-stage dynamics). Mitigate with time-windowing and causal training. (4) Incorrect equilibrium states — the network may converge to spurious steady states not at φ = ±1. Validate against known analytical solutions (e.g., 1D tanh profile for equilibrium interfaces). (5) Energy non-monotonicity — if energy increases during training, the model violates thermodynamics. Monitor F(t) as a diagnostic. (6) Missing parameter specifications mean the workflow cannot be finalized; incorrect assumptions about M, κ, or boundary conditions will produce physically wrong solutions.

model_answer: A Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) with a two-network or combined architecture is recommended. Option A (stream function formulation): A single network ψ_θ(x,y,t) outputs the stream function, with u and v obtained via automatic differentiation; a second network p_θ(x,y,t) outputs pressure. This exactly satisfies incompressibility, reducing PDE residuals to the momentum equations only, and is preferred for its constraint satisfaction. Option B (primitive variable formulation): A single network outputs (u,v,p) jointly, with incompressibility enforced as a soft penalty—simpler but less robust. The stream function approach is justified because: (1) it eliminates the continuity constraint entirely, reducing loss balancing difficulty; (2) it naturally handles the pressure-velocity coupling; (3) it avoids spurious divergence in regions far from data. Network architecture: modified MLP with Fourier feature embeddings or sinusoidal activations to capture multi-scale flow features, width 128–256, depth 6–8. For higher Reynolds numbers, consider domain decomposition or extended PINN architectures with adaptive activation functions.

Sampling: Use Latin Hypercube or quasi-random (Sobol) sampling for collocation points in Ω×[0,T], with denser sampling near boundaries and in regions with observations. Resample periodically (every N epochs) to avoid overfitting to fixed collocation points. Loss weighting: Employ adaptive weighting (e.g., GradNorm, Neural Tangent Kernel-based, or learning-rate-based balancing) since momentum residuals and data terms can differ by orders of magnitude. Optimization: Adam optimizer with learning rate 10⁻³ decaying via cosine annealing, followed by L-BFGS fine-tuning for precise PDE satisfaction. Training proceeds in two phases: (1) pretrain on data and BC/IC losses to establish a reasonable velocity field; (2) add PDE residual losses with ramped-up weights. For stiff or high-Re problems, use causal training (time-marching) or curriculum learning on Reynolds number.

model_answer: A modified MLP with Fourier feature encoding (Random Fourier Features or sinusoidal positional embeddings) is recommended, specifically a multi-scale Fourier feature network. Justification: (1) Standard MLPs suffer severe spectral bias and cannot resolve oscillations when kL is large; Fourier features provide explicit high-frequency basis functions that break this bias. (2) The Helmholtz equation is linear and elliptic, so an operator-approximation approach is natural, but the high-frequency regime makes FNOs expensive in spectral bandwidth. (3) A phase-amplitude decomposition u = A·exp(iS) is an alternative, where A (amplitude) and S (phase) are learned separately; this respects the eikonal structure at high frequency and reduces the effective frequency the network must represent, but introduces nonlinearity and branch-cut issues in S. Recommendation: use the phase-amplitude ansatz as the primary approach with A and S parameterized by separate Fourier-feature MLPs, falling back to direct complex-field representation if the geometry causes caustics or multipathing where the single-phase ansatz breaks. Both Re(u) and Im(u) can also be learned as two real outputs of one network with Fourier features, which is simpler but less sample-efficient at high k.

(4) Phase wrapping in the phase-amplitude ansatz: the network may produce discontinuous S, leading to gradient errors; monitor S smoothness and consider using sin(S), cos(S) parameterization instead. (5) Boundary layer errors: high-frequency Helmholtz solutions often have boundary layers (evanescent waves) that are under-sampled; use adaptive collocation near ∂Ω. (6) Overfitting to collocation points: with many points needed for resolution, regularization (weight decay, dropout) must be carefully tuned. (7) Validation metric insensitivity: L² error can be small even when phase error is large (cancellation); always report separate amplitude and phase errors, and pointwise |u| error. (8) Missing BC type: if the boundary condition type is mis-specified (e.g., Dirichlet assumed but Robin needed), the solution will be qualitatively wrong; this is an irreducible risk until BCs are confirmed.

model_answer: A standard PINN with a plain MLP is insufficient for this problem for three reasons: (a) spectral bias prevents learning high-frequency oscillations when k is large; (b) sharp corners require localized refinement that uniform networks cannot provide; (c) the strong-form collocation loss for Helmholtz is known to be poorly conditioned at high k. Recommended model: a Multi-Scale Fourier Feature Network (MS-FFN) with domain decomposition. Specifically: (i) Use random Fourier feature embeddings with frequencies sampled from a distribution spanning [0, k] to match the oscillation scale, breaking spectral bias. (ii) Partition the staircase domain into subdomains aligned with stair steps using XPINN/cPINN-style decomposition, assigning separate networks per subdomain with interface continuity conditions. (iii) At corners, introduce local enrichment networks or adaptive activation functions. (iv) Consider a variational (weak-form) PINN loss instead of strong-form collocation, as it is better conditioned for Helmholtz and naturally handles corner singularities through integration by parts. Justification: Fourier features directly address the frequency mismatch; domain decomposition localizes corner difficulties; variational form reduces derivative order sensitivity.

Justification: (1) No simulation or measurement data is specified, ruling out pure data-driven or operator approaches; PINNs solve the forward problem from PDE constraints alone. (4) Domain decomposition (XPINN) is strongly motivated: partition the maze into corridors/rooms at narrow passages, assign sub-networks, and enforce interface continuity. (5) A PML region surrounding the domain is appended to the network's spatial domain with the complex-stretched Helmholtz residual as an additional loss term. Alternative considered: FEM with PML is more reliable for production but does not meet the physics-informed ML workflow requirement; BEM handles scattering naturally but scales poorly with the many wall elements in a maze.

model_answer: (1) Spectral bias at high k: the network may fail to capture fine interference fringes from multiple wall reflections. Mitigation: Fourier features, k-curriculum, and sufficient network width/depth. (2) Wall BC violation in narrow corridors: thin passages have closely spaced walls where Neumann conditions on both sides compete, causing gradient conflicts. Mitigation: oversample narrow corridors, use hard BC enforcement where possible, and monitor wall loss separately. (3) PML spurious reflections: improperly tuned PML thickness or stretching profile reflects energy back into the domain. Mitigation: validate against analytical solutions for simple scatterers with the same PML; sweep PML parameters. (4) Mode trapping / resonance: if k coincides with a cavity resonance within the maze, the solution is ill-conditioned (near-singular). Mitigation: add small damping or verify k is off-resonance; check condition number of FEM stiffness matrix at the same k. (5) XPINN interface discontinuities: poor interface enforcement creates unphysical scattering at sub-domain boundaries. Mitigation: use overlap regions and strong interface penalties; verify interface flux continuity as a validation metric. (6) Reciprocity violation: train-network solutions may not satisfy reciprocity. Mitigation: compute scattered field for swapped source/receiver and check agreement — violation indicates insufficient convergence. (7) Missing geometry detail: if the SDF is computed from an incomplete wall description, spurious transmission through walls occurs. Mitigation: cross-check SDF against the full boundary description; verify zero penetration via point-in-polygon tests.

model_answer: The governing equations are the 1D compressible Euler equations in conservation form: ∂U/∂t + ∂F(U)/∂x = 0, where U = [ρ, ρu, E]^T and F(U) = [ρu, ρu²+p, u(E+p)]^T. The equation of state closes the system: p = (γ−1)(E − ½ρu²). The initial condition is piecewise constant (Riemann-type), e.g., U(x,0) = U_L for x<x₀ and U_R for x>x₀. The spatial domain, boundary conditions, specific initial states (U_L, U_R), the ratio of specific heats γ, and the spatial dimensionality are not specified; these must be provided to complete the formalization. Without them, shock speed, rarefaction fan structure, and contact location cannot be determined analytically for validation.

(2) Rankine-Hugoniot jump conditions relating states across shocks: s[U] = [F(U)], where s is the shock speed. (3) Entropy inequality (Lax entropy condition): physical shocks must be compressive, with characteristics converging into the shock. (4) Contact discontinuities preserve pressure and velocity while allowing density jumps. (5) The ideal gas equation of state (assumed; γ must be specified). Standard pointwise PDE residuals are ill-defined at discontinuities, so weak-form or entropy-regularized constraints are essential.

model_answer: A weak-form physics-informed neural network (wPINN) or entropy-compatible PINN is recommended. Justification: (1) The Euler equations are hyperbolic with discontinuous solutions; pointwise collocation fails at shocks due to undefined derivatives. (2) The weak (integral) form naturally accommodates discontinuities via integration by parts, shifting derivatives onto smooth test functions. (3) An entropy variable formulation (e.g., using the entropy η = −ρs with s = cv ln(p/ρ^γ)) ensures convergence to the physically correct entropy-satisfying solution and excludes rarefaction shocks. (4) Domain decomposition with separate networks per smooth region (separated by learned shock/contact interfaces) can further improve accuracy. A fully connected network with moderate depth (6–8 layers, 64–128 neurons) and tanh/sin activations is a reasonable base architecture.

model_answer: The system is governed by the 2D incompressible Navier-Stokes equations under the Boussinesq approximation, coupling velocity (u, v), pressure (p), and temperature (T). Non-dimensionalized using cell height H, temperature difference ΔT, and thermal diffusion time H²/κ, the equations are: (1) ∂u/∂t + (u·∇)u = -∇p + Pr·∇²u + Ra·Pr·T·ŷ (momentum), (2) ∇·u = 0 (incompressibility), (3) ∂T/∂t + (u·∇)T = ∇²T (energy). Key outputs are the Nusselt number Nu (integrated heat flux) and velocity/temperature fields. Missing specifications: aspect ratio Γ = L/H (affects roll selection and wavenumber), Rayleigh number Ra = gαΔTH³/(νκ) (determines convective vigor and boundary layer thickness), Prandtl number Pr = ν/κ (affects velocity-temperature coupling strength), sidewall boundary conditions (no-slip vs. free-slip, insulating vs. conducting), top/bottom thermal BCs (fixed-T vs. fixed-flux), initial conditions, and whether steady or time-dependent solutions are sought. These gaps critically affect expected flow patterns, boundary layer resolution requirements, and validation targets.

model_answer: A Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) with a single modified MLP backbone outputting (u, v, p, T) is recommended. Justification: (1) The coupled elliptic-parabolic PDE system with strong nonlinear advection suits residual-based training; (2) Incompressibility is enforced as a soft constraint via the divergence residual, avoiding saddle-point issues of mixed formulations; (3) No large simulation dataset is assumed available, making purely data-driven operators (FNO, DeepONet) infeasible. Architecture details: 8–10 layers, 128–256 units/layer, sin activations or Fourier feature embeddings for spectral bias toward high-frequency boundary layers. For high Ra (>10⁶), a domain decomposition approach (e.g., XPINNs or overlapping subdomains) is necessary because single-network PINNs fail to resolve thin thermal boundary layers of thickness ~H/(2Nu). Alternative: if DNS/experimental data exists, a hybrid PINN with data-matching terms should be used. For time-dependent problems at high Ra, a sequential time-windowing strategy (causal training) is essential.

model_answer: (1) Boundary layer under-resolution: at high Ra, thermal boundary layers scale as ~Ra^(-1/3), requiring exponentially more collocation points; failure manifests as Nu underestimation and smeared temperature gradients near walls. Mitigation: adaptive collocation refinement guided by residual magnitude. (2) Mode selection ambiguity: without specified initial conditions, the network may converge to any allowable convective roll pattern; multiple solutions exist. Mitigation: specify initial perturbation or validate against a particular mode. (3) Pressure indeterminacy: without a pressure gauge point, the pressure field drifts, causing slow convergence. Mitigation: enforce p=0 at one point. (4) Training instability at high Ra: stiff coupling between buoyancy and advection causes gradient explosion. Mitigation: Ra-curriculum training and gradient clipping. (5) Spurious steady states: the network may find a conduction-only solution (u≈0, T linear) if the loss landscape traps it. Mitigation: initialize with a small convective perturbation or pre-train on a known convective solution. (6) Validation metrics must include: Nu convergence (compare against Glazier et al. scaling Nu ~ 0.069·Ra^(1/3) for moderate Ra), velocity field divergence (∥∇·u∥ should be <10⁻⁴), and temperature profile matching against benchmark DNS data (e.g., from the 2D RB benchmark datasets). Failure to match Nu within 5% indicates inadequate boundary layer resolution.

model_answer: The 2D resistive MHD system couples incompressible fluid momentum and magnetic induction. State variables: velocity field u=(u_x,u_y), magnetic field B=(B_x,B_y), pressure p, and scalar density ρ. Governing equations: (1) Momentum: ρ(∂u/∂t + u·∇u) = -∇p + J×B + ν∇²u + f_ext, where J=∇×B is current density; (2) Induction: ∂B/∂t = ∇×(u×B) + η∇²B; (3) Incompressibility: ∇·u = 0; (4) Solenoidal constraint: ∇·B = 0. model_answer: Primary constraints: (1) ∇·B = 0 must hold exactly or to machine precision—violations produce unphysical magnetic monopoles and spurious forces; (2) ∇·u = 0 for incompressibility; (3) Energy dissipation rate must be non-negative: dE/dt ≤ 0 in absence of forcing, where E = ∫(½ρ|u|² + ½|B|²)dx; (4) Boundary conditions (unspecified) must be enforced consistently. The divergence-free constraints are the most critical; penalty-based enforcement alone is insufficient for strong coupling regimes. Vector potential formulation (B = ∇×A with A = A_z ẑ in 2D, so B_x = ∂A_z/∂y, B_y = -∂A_z/∂x) guarantees ∇·B = 0 by construction. Similarly, stream function ψ for velocity (u_x = ∂ψ/∂y, u_y = -∂ψ/∂x) guarantees ∇·u = 0.

model_answer: Use a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) with divergence-free architectural hard constraints. Represent B via a scalar magnetic potential A_z(x,y,t) and u via a stream function ψ(x,y,t), each parameterized by separate neural networks (or shared trunk with separate heads). Pressure p(x,y,t) is a separate network output. This guarantees ∇·B = 0 and ∇·u = 0 exactly by construction, eliminating the most common failure mode. Justification: (1) the PDE system is coupled and nonlinear with Lorentz force J×B coupling back to momentum—PINNs handle strong nonlinear coupling via automatic differentiation; (2) sparse observations are naturally incorporated as data loss terms; (3) the vector potential/stream function formulation removes the need for soft divergence penalties, which are unreliable at high magnetic Reynolds numbers. Network architecture: modified MLP with Fourier feature embeddings for spectral bias mitigation, 4–6 hidden layers, 128–256 units, sin/Swish activations. Separate networks for (A_z, ψ, p) or a multi-output network with physically motivated output heads.

model_answer: Loss function: L = λ_pde·L_pde + λ_data·L_data + λ_bc·L_bc + λ_ic·L_ic + λ_div·L_div_monitor. L_pde comprises residuals of momentum (x,y components) and induction equations computed via automatic differentiation of A_z and ψ. L_data = MSE at sparse observation points. L_bc and L_ic enforce boundary and initial conditions (must be provided). L_div_monitor = (∇·B)² + (∇·u)² is a diagnostic-only term (should be zero by construction; nonzero values indicate numerical differentiation errors). Sampling: Latin hypercube or Sobol sequence for spatiotemporal collocation points, with adaptive refinement near high-residual regions (residual-based adaptive sampling). Optimization: two-phase—Adam (lr=1e-3) for initial convergence, then L-BFGS for fine-tuning. Stabilization: (1) curriculum learning—gradually increase coupling strength (start with decoupled hydro+induction, ramp up Lorentz force and induction coupling); (2) causal training—weight temporal residuals to respect causality; (3) gradient clipping to prevent exploding gradients from J×B nonlinearities. λ weights tuned via gradient balancing (e.g., GradNorm or learning rate annealing) to prevent PDE residual terms from dominating data terms or vice versa.

model_answer: (1) Divergence constraint violation: even with vector potential formulation, numerical differentiation errors can introduce small ∇·B; monitor continuously and switch to higher-precision automatic differentiation if violations exceed 1e-6. (2) Stiff velocity-magnetic coupling: at high magnetic Reynolds numbers (Rm = UL/η ≫ 1) or high Lundquist numbers, the Lorentz force and induction terms create stiffness; the curriculum strategy may still fail—fallback to operator splitting or time-marching PINN variants. (3) Missing boundary/initial conditions: without these, the inverse problem is underdetermined; the workflow must detect and report this, and cannot proceed to training without at least boundary condition specification. (4) Pressure indeterminacy: in incompressible MHD, pressure is determined only up to a constant; enforce a pressure gauge condition (e.g., p=0 at one point) or the pressure network may drift. (5) Spectral bias: MHD solutions often contain fine current sheets and boundary layers; Fourier features help but may be insufficient—validate against high-resolution spectral solutions on benchmark problems (e.g., Orszag-Tang vortex, lid-driven MHD cavity). (6) Observation sparsity: if observations are too sparse or clustered, the inverse problem for unknown parameters (ν, η) is ill-conditioned; validate with posterior uncertainty quantification or ensemble methods.

pde_system: Multiscale Darcy flow with high-contrast permeability
model_answer: Single-instance solver: Use a multiscale PINN with domain decomposition or a finite element method (FEM) with adaptive mesh refinement near channels. The PINN residual loss enforces −∇·(k∇h) = f directly; a hard constraint or penalty enforces BCs. Multiscale architecture (e.g., hierarchical Fourier features or separate subnetworks per subdomain) captures channel-scale and domain-scale features. Reusable surrogate: Use a Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) or factorized FNO that maps (k, f) → h, conditioned on BCs via embedding. The operator must be resolution-invariant to handle varying channel fineness. Justification: FNOs handle parameterized PDE families well, respect translational equivariance, and generalize across coefficient fields. For both cases, the high-contrast k requires either (a) log-transformed input k̃ = log(k) to reduce dynamic range, or (b) multiscale input encoding. A pure PINN surrogate across many k-fields is less efficient than an FNO because it lacks weight sharing across realizations.

pde_system: Multiscale Darcy flow with high-contrast permeability
model_answer: (1) High-contrast stiffness: standard PINNs fail to resolve sharp head gradients at channel–matrix interfaces, producing smoothed solutions that violate flux continuity. Mitigation: adaptive sampling and multiscale architectures. (2) Surrogate generalization gap: FNO trained on limited k-realizations may fail on out-of-distribution channel geometries or higher contrast ratios. Mitigation: diverse training set spanning channel topology and contrast range; test on extrapolative cases. (3) Spectral bias: neural networks under-represent high-frequency channel features. Mitigation: Fourier feature embeddings, multi-scale loss weighting. (4) Conservation violation: predicted h may not satisfy mass balance, especially far from training data. Mitigation: add divergence-free projection or hard conservation constraints. Alternatively, a physics-informed neural network with adaptive activation functions or multi-scale architectures (e.g., MscalePINN) can improve convergence.

model_answer: Key risks: (1) The model may fail to capture sharp discontinuities due to spectral bias, leading to smeared shocks or oscillations (Gibbs phenomenon). (4) The model may overfit to collocation points if sampling is not adaptive. (5) Without specified boundary conditions, the problem is ill-posed; validation against incomplete data may be misleading. Mitigations: use adaptive collocation, entropy loss, conservative formulations, and validate against exact or high-fidelity numerical solutions.

An alternative is a soft penalty approach with adaptive weighting, but hard constraints are preferred when BC/IC are well-defined.

Weights may be tuned via adaptive schemes (e.g., grad norm balancing) to avoid dominance by any one term, especially when v is large relative to D (advection-dominated regime). Optimization begins with Adam (learning rate ~1e-3) for global search, followed by L-BFGS for fine-tuning. The model is trained until the PDE residual and data mismatch fall below a threshold or plateau. Mini-batching is optional but can be used if the sampling is dense enough.

This can be mitigated by increased collocation density near fronts, adaptive sampling, or specialized architectures. 2. Loss balancing: If the PDE residual term overwhelms data terms (or vice versa), the model may satisfy the PDE but not the initial/boundary conditions, or may be biased toward data and ignore physics. Adaptive weighting is essential. 3. Insufficient spatial/temporal resolution: Sparse collocation points can cause inaccurate advection tracking or miss boundary layer effects. Validation against an analytical solution (if available) or a high-fidelity numerical solver is needed. 4. Violation of maximum principle: The PINN may predict unphysical negative concentrations or values beyond bounds if not constrained. Regularization or output transformation (e.g., softplus) may be needed if concentration must be non-negative. 5. Long-time integration: Error accumulation over time can degrade predictions; training with time-marching schemes or curriculum learning may help.

Mitigation requires careful sampling, loss scheduling, and possibly adding artificial diffusion during training.

model_answer: 1. Mass drift: if the mass conservation penalty is too weak or integration inaccurate, total mass may change over time, violating the core physics. 2. Failure to resolve sharp interfaces: standard PINNs with uniform sampling may produce diffuse or oscillatory interfaces, leading to incorrect phase evolution. 3. Energy increase: if the network violates the dissipation inequality, the solution may be non-physical (e.g., uphill diffusion). 4. Stiffness from small ε: the PDE becomes singularly perturbed; PINNs may fail to converge or require excessive collocation points. 5. Boundary condition mismatch: if no-flux conditions are not strictly enforced, mass may leak through boundaries. 6. Mobility positivity: if M(φ) is modeled as a neural network, it may become negative, causing anti-diffusion and instability. Mitigation: use monotonicity constraints or explicit positive functions for M.

model_answer: The task is to approximate solutions of the compressible Euler equations for inviscid flow, where the initial state is piecewise constant, leading to possible shocks, contact discontinuities, and rarefactions. Governing PDEs in conservative form (1D): ∂_t ρ + ∂_x (ρu) = 0, ∂_t (ρu) + ∂_x (ρu^2 + p) = 0, ∂_t E + ∂_x (u(E+p)) = 0, with ideal gas law p = (γ-1)(E - 0.5ρu^2). The domain is not specified; we assume a 1D spatial interval [0, L] and a time interval [0, T]. Initial conditions (IC) are piecewise states but exact values, positions, and boundary conditions (BC) are missing. Without domain size, final time, and specific IC/BC, we can only design a generic workflow; we assume periodic or transmissive BCs to mimic a Riemann problem on a finite domain. The outputs are fields ρ(x,t), u(x,t) (or momentum m), and p(x,t) (or energy E). The solution is expected to contain discontinuities, requiring a workflow that can handle non-smooth functions.

model_answer: Conservation laws must hold in weak form at discontinuities. In smooth regions, the differential form applies. We enforce the residuals of the three conservation equations via automatic differentiation. To select physically admissible shocks, an entropy condition (e.g., δ_s/δ_t + δ_(su)/δ_x ≥ 0, s = ln(p ρ^{-γ})) is added as a soft constraint via a penalty loss on negative entropy production. Additional constraints: positivity of density and pressure (implemented via output transformations). The Rankine-Hugoniot jump conditions are not directly imposed; the network approximates the weak solution implicitly if the PDE residual is minimized sufficiently over regions containing shocks, but adaptive sampling near jumps is critical. Missing specific BCs and ICs means we must parameterize the loss terms with placeable constraints; in practice, one would encode the given piecewise initial state as label data at t=0 and impose boundary conditions (e.g., Dirichlet or periodic) at x boundaries.

model_answer: Training is purely physics-driven unless supplemented by data. Collocation points are initially sampled uniformly in the interior of the space-time domain and on boundaries. Adaptive refinement is key: every N iterations, additional points are added where PDE residual magnitude is largest. Loss function: L(θ) = λ_PDE Σ₍_(PDE residuals)^2 + λ_IC Σ_(IC mismatch)^2 + λ_BC Σ_(BC mismatch)^2 + λ_entropy Σ max(0, -∂_t s - ∂_x(s u))^2. Optimization uses AdamW for initial rapid convergence, followed by L‑BFGS for fine-tuning. To avoid overly smooth shock profiles, a total variation penalty on spatial gradients can be added, or a temperature scaling in the activation. Training is computationally intensive; minibatch sampling and transfer learning from a coarser solution can accelerate convergence. Validation metrics include L² error against a reference finite volume solution, shock propagation speed, and overshoot magnitude.

If the IC and BC are not provided, the validation scenario is not fully defined, limiting the robustness guarantee. Mitigations include using adaptive sampling, entropy loss, and comparing with characteristic-based exact solvers.

model_answer: We model 2D Rayleigh-Bénard convection in a rectangular cell of height H and width L. The fluid satisfies the Oberbeck–Boussinesq equations: continuity ∇·u=0, momentum ∂u/∂t + u·∇u = −∇p + ν∇²u + gβ(T−T0) e_y, and energy ∂T/∂t + u·∇T = α∇²T. Unknown fields are velocity u=(u,v), pressure p, and temperature T. The bottom wall is isothermal at T_h, top wall at T_c (T_h>T_c), sidewalls adiabatic. No-slip conditions on all walls. Initial condition is quiescent fluid with a linear conduction temperature profile. Key missing specifications: Rayleigh number Ra, Prandtl number Pr, aspect ratio Γ=L/H, and precise initial perturbation (if any). Their absence prevents fixing length/time scales and may alter the convective regime.

High-contrast K leads to sharp gradients in h and localized high velocities in channels. The solution must satisfy the enforced boundary conditions (e.g., h|∂Ω_D = g_D, –K∇h·n|∂Ω_N = g_N). The multiscale nature requires that the model resolve both coarse pressure trends and fine channel effects. Mass conservation is a strict physical constraint, and fluxes should be consistent with Darcy's law. In surrogate modeling, any learned mapping must respect these PDE constraints for all admissible K fields, not just on training data.

model_answer: 1) Ill-posedness from missing BCs/IC: if boundary or initial conditions are not obtained and are instead jointly inferred, α may be non-identifiable—multiple (α, BC, IC) combinations can fit sparse data equally well, leading to physically incorrect diffusivity. 2) Sparse data aliasing: if observations are too sparse relative to the spatial/temporal frequency content of T, the network may fit noise or produce unphysical oscillations between measurement points, corrupting α estimation. 3) Gradient imbalance: PDE loss gradients with respect to network weights may dominate or vanish relative to data loss gradients, causing the optimizer to satisfy the PDE with a trivial solution (e.g., T≈constant) while ignoring data, or vice versa. 4) Noise-induced bias: systematic noise or outliers in T_obs can bias α if data loss is over-weighted; robust loss functions (Huber) may be needed. 5) Stiffness in optimization: the loss landscape for inverse problems is often non-convex with local minima where α converges to incorrect values; multi-start initialization of α_raw is recommended. 6) Extrapolation failure: reconstruction quality degrades far from observation points, especially near boundaries if BCs are uncertain.

Optimization: Adam optimizer (lr ~ 1e-3) for initial convergence, then L-BFGS for fine-tuning. Loss balancing: use adaptive weighting (e.g., learning rate annealing or NTK-based gradient balancing) to prevent PDE residual from dominating or being dominated by noisy data. Parameterization: ν = exp(θ_ν) to enforce positivity. Observations consist of sparse point measurements {u(x_i, t_j)} at N_s sensor locations. The initial conditions u(x, 0) = f(x) and u_t(x, 0) = g(x) are partially or fully unspecified. Boundary conditions at x = 0 and x = L (Dirichlet, Neumann, or absorbing) are also partially or fully unspecified. This makes the problem a joint state-reconstruction and data-assimilation task. Missing ICs and BCs introduce non-uniqueness: without sufficient constraints, infinitely many solutions satisfy the PDE and the sparse observations. Identifiability requires that the sensor density and placement, combined with the known c, provide enough information to resolve the characteristic lines and distinguish propagating modes. Specifically, the sensor spacing Δx must satisfy a Nyquist-type criterion relative to the highest resolvable frequency, and sensors must be placed such that their backward characteristic cones adequately cover the domain.

Mitigation: spectral analysis of residuals and explicit band-limiting assumption. 2) Non-uniqueness from missing BCs/ICs: the optimizer may converge to a solution that fits data and PDE but has unphysical reflections or energy injection at boundaries. Mitigation: test with assumed absorbing BCs and check for spurious boundary reflections. 3) Spectral bias: the network may fail to learn high-frequency wave components, producing overly smooth solutions. Mitigation: Fourier feature embeddings and monitoring frequency content of predictions vs. data. 4) Causality violation: without causality-aware training, errors at early times propagate and amplify, corrupting the entire solution. Mitigation: causal training curriculum. 5) Overfitting to sparse sensors: with few data points and many network parameters, the model may interpolate noise. Mitigation: early stopping, L2 regularization, and cross-validation on held-out sensors. 6) Stiff optimization landscape: competing loss terms (data vs. PDE) can cause gradient pathology. Mitigation: NTK-based adaptive weighting and sequential training strategies.

(3) Causality violation — the network may fit later-time solutions before properly resolving early-time dynamics, especially with uniform interior sampling; mitigated by causal training (sequentially revealing time windows) or time-dependent loss weighting. (4) Stiffness at boundary junctions — the corner point (x = L, t = 0) where Neumann BC meets the initial condition can create conflicting gradient signals if f'(L) ≠ −q(0)/k, causing localized oscillations. (5) Parameter sensitivity — if α or k are uncertain or incorrectly specified, the model will converge to a physically inconsistent solution; a parameter identification extension with observational data would be needed. (6) Long-time drift — for large T, errors accumulate and the solution may violate the maximum principle; mitigated by time-marching decomposition.

Without these, the model architecture, sampling strategy, and loss weighting cannot be fully determined.

model_answer: A Physics-Informed Neural Operator (PINO) based on a Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) backbone with a conservative output layer. Justification: (1) FNO efficiently handles the spatial differential operators via spectral convolutions, naturally suited for the high-order derivatives in Cahn-Hilliard. (2) Operator learning maps initial conditions to solutions, enabling generalization across different φ(x,0) without retraining. (3) The mixed formulation (two coupled second-order equations for φ and μ) avoids direct fourth-order derivative computation, which is numerically unstable in automatic differentiation. (4) A conservative projection layer enforces ∫φ dV = const by construction (subtracting the mean drift at each time step). (5) For sharp interfaces, standard FNO spectral truncation causes Gibbs-like oscillations; therefore, a hybrid approach with a U-Net branch in physical space captures localized sharp gradients while FNO handles global smooth dynamics. Alternative considered: standard PINN rejected due to poor scaling with 4th-order derivatives and inability to generalize across initial conditions.

model_answer: Primary risk is phase error accumulation: even a small local phase shift propagates and causes large global L² errors, making relative L² error an unreliable sole metric—validate amplitude error ‖|u_pred| − |u_ref|‖ and phase error ‖arg(u_pred) − arg(u_ref)‖ separately. Second risk is the pollution effect: as k increases, the required collocation density grows super-linearly, and under-resolution leads to numerical dispersion and wrong wave speeds. Third risk is mode collapse or convergence to a trivial/spurious solution, especially if the source term f is localized and the network learns a near-zero solution that minimizes residual in most of the domain. Fourth risk is overfitting to collocation points with poor interpolation between them; mitigate by evaluating on a dense independent test grid. Fifth risk: if boundary condition type is mis-specified or incompatible with the source term, the problem may be ill-posed, leading to non-convergence. Validation metrics should include: (1) relative L² error on a dense grid, (2) maximum pointwise phase error, (3) mean amplitude error, (4) energy conservation check (integrated flux balance), and (5) visual comparison of wavefronts against a reference FEM/BEM solution.

model_answer: A standard PINN with a plain MLP is insufficient for three reasons: (1) spectral bias—MLPs preferentially learn low-frequency functions and cannot efficiently represent the O(k²) oscillatory modes; (2) indefiniteness—the non-convex Helmholtz loss landscape causes standard gradient descent to stagnate; (3) corner singularities—smooth activations (tanh, sin) cannot capture the r^α singular gradients at re-entrant corners. Recommended architecture: a domain-decomposed Fourier-feature PINN (XPINN or similar). Specifically: (a) partition the staircase into rectangular subdomains, each handled by a separate sub-network; (b) use learnable Fourier feature embeddings (or SIREN with high ω₀) as input encoding to overcome spectral bias; (c) enforce interface continuity (u and ∂u/∂n) between subdomains via penalty terms; (d) near re-entrant corners, either enrich the basis with known singular functions (e.g., r^α sin(αθ)) or use an extremely refined local subdomain with a dedicated high-capacity network. This hybrid approach addresses all three failure modes of standard PINNs.

Pollution effect: Even with adequate sampling, the relative error in Helmholtz solutions grows as k increases (error ∝ k^p h^q), so validation at a single k may not reveal degradation at higher k. Mitigate by testing across a range of k values. 2. Mode skipping / wrong branch: The indefinite Helmholtz equation has many near-resonant modes; the network may converge to a spurious mode that has low residual but is physically incorrect. Mitigate by comparing against a coarse FEM solution or analytical benchmarks on simplified sub-geometries. 3. Corner singularity under-resolution: If the singular gradient at re-entrant corners is not captured, local errors will pollute the global solution. Validate by checking convergence of the energy norm near corners under mesh refinement. 4. Spectral bias residual: Fourier features may still damp the highest-frequency components. Validate by computing the power spectrum of the predicted solution and comparing against expected spectral content. 5. Interface artifacts: Domain decomposition may introduce spurious reflections or discontinuities at subdomain boundaries. Validate by checking flux continuity and solution smoothness across interfaces. 6. Overfitting to collocation points: The network may interpolate training points without generalizing. Validate using a held-out set of randomly sampled test points and report L² and H¹ relative errors.

The total pressure field p = p_inc + p_scat satisfies the Helmholtz equation ∇²p + k²p = 0 in the fluid region. The incident wave p_inc is given; the scattered field p_scat must satisfy a Sommerfeld radiation condition (or PML-truncated equivalent) at the outer boundary. Obstacle wall boundary conditions are unspecified (sound-hard Neumann ∂p/∂n=0, sound-soft Dirichlet p=0, or impedance Robin), and must be clarified before implementation. The wavenumber k, obstacle geometry representation (CAD, mesh, or implicit SDF), and whether obstacles are penetrable (transmission problem) or impenetrable are also unspecified and critically affect domain decomposition and interface conditions. The workflow assumes impenetrable obstacles with a single boundary condition type until clarified.

model_answer: Domain-decomposed Physics-Informed Neural Network (XPINN) with Fourier feature embeddings. Justification: (a) The maze geometry naturally partitions into subdomains (corridors, junctions), and XPINN handles complex boundaries by assigning separate networks per subdomain with interface continuity losses. (b) Fourier features (random or trainable frequency mappings) mitigate spectral bias inherent to high-wavenumber Helmholtz solutions, which standard MLPs severely underfit. (c) Unlike FNO, this approach does not require a large precomputed dataset of solutions across geometries. (d) Boundary Element-informed alternatives were considered but struggle with multiple scattering in maze interiors where volumetric PDE residuals provide better constraint. Each subdomain network uses a modified MLP with sinusoidal activation (SIREN) or Fourier-feature input layer, outputting real and imaginary parts of p.

model_answer: Loss = λ_pde * L_Helmholtz + λ_bc * L_boundary + λ_if * L_interface + λ_rad * L_radiation. L_Helmholtz: MSE of ∇²p + k²p over collocation points sampled via Sobol sequences with adaptive refinement near walls and corners (residual-based importance sampling). L_boundary: MSE of BC residual on obstacle surfaces. L_interface: continuity of p and ∂p/∂n across subdomain boundaries. L_radiation: PML absorption loss or Sommerfeld residual on outer boundary. Optimization: Adam (lr=1e-3, 20k steps) followed by L-BFGS (5k steps) for sharp convergence. Curriculum: train at low k first, then progressively increase wavenumber, transferring weights. Hard-constraint the incident field analytically and only learn p_scat to reduce solution space. Use NTK-guidate weighting to balance loss term magnitudes. Sampling density must scale as O(k²) per wavelength to resolve oscillations.

model_answer: 1) Spectral bias at high k: Fourier features may still fail if k is very large (>50), producing overly smooth solutions that miss higher-order scattering modes. Mitigation: validate against BEM reference at select frequencies. 2) Spurious interior resonances: at certain k values, the Helmholtz BVP becomes ill-conditioned (near eigenfrequencies of enclosed subdomains), causing non-physical amplification. Mitigation: use combined-field integral equation formulation or CHIEF-point stabilization. 3) Corner singularities: sharp maze corners produce unbounded gradients that neural networks cannot resolve, leading to local error hotspots and polluted global solution. Mitigation: singularity subtraction or graded mesh refinement. 4) PML reflections: imperfect absorbing layers cause artificial standing waves, especially at grazing incidence along maze corridors. Mitigation: validate energy flux balance. 5) Multiple scattering error accumulation: each wall reflection compounds phase errors, degrading deep-interior accuracy. Mitigation: domain decomposition limits error propagation per subdomain. 6) Unspecified BC type: wrong BC assumption invalidates entire solution; must confirm with acoustics group before training.

model_answer: Model the 1D compressible Euler equations in conservation form: ∂U/∂t + ∂F(U)/∂x = 0, where U = [ρ, ρu, E]ᵀ and F(U) = [ρu, ρu² + p, u(E + p)]ᵀ, closed by the ideal-gas equation of state p = (γ − 1)(E − ½ρu²). Initial conditions are piecewise constant (Riemann-type), generating shocks, contact discontinuities, and rarefaction waves. Missing specifications that must be declared before execution: (1) the ratio of specific heats γ (commonly 1.4 for air), (2) the spatial domain [x_L, x_R] and final time T, (3) boundary conditions (e.g., transmissive/outflow vs. reflective), (4) the exact left and right states (ρ_L, u_L, p_L) and (ρ_R, u_R, p_R) and the initial discontinuity location x₀, and (5) whether the problem is strictly 1D or extends to multi-D. These missing items directly affect shock speeds, wave interactions, and the loss-function design.

model_answer: 1. Integral (weak) conservation of mass, momentum, and energy must hold across all subdomains, especially across discontinuities where classical derivatives are undefined. 2. Rankine-Hugoniot jump conditions must be satisfied at shock and contact locations, relating shock speed to flux jumps. 3. Entropy admissibility condition (e.g., Lax entropy condition or an entropy inequality ∂(ρs)/∂t + ∂(ρus)/∂x ≥ 0) to exclude non-physical expansion shocks. 4. Positivity constraints: ρ > 0 and p > 0 everywhere at all times. 5. Total variation boundedness (TVB) of the solution to prevent spurious oscillations near sharp fronts. 6. Equation of state consistency linking pressure, density, and internal energy at every evaluation point.

model_answer: A Conservative Physics-Informed Neural Network (cPINN) with domain decomposition, combined with a weak-form residual loss. Rationale: (a) Standard strong-form PINNs fail on discontinuous solutions because pointwise PDE residuals blow up at shocks and contacts. (b) The cPINN partitions the spatiotemporal domain into subdomains separated by learned or tracked discontinuity curves; within each smooth subdomain a standard MLP represents U, while flux continuity (Rankine-Hugoniot) is enforced at interfaces. (c) The weak-form loss integrates test functions against the residual over local space-time patches, naturally accommodating discontinuities without requiring pointwise differentiability. (d) An auxiliary small network or level-set function tracks shock/contact locations to guide domain decomposition. (e) Positivity of ρ and p is enforced via softplus or exponential parameterization of the network outputs for these quantities. Alternative considered and rejected: pure Fourier Neural Operator—while powerful for smooth problems, it suffers from Gibbs oscillations near sharp fronts unless supplemented with explicit shock-capturing, making cPINN more directly suited to this task.

model_answer: Loss function comprises five terms: (i) weak-form PDE residual loss integrated over space-time patches using quadrature with piecewise-linear test functions; (ii) initial-condition loss enforcing piecewise states at t = 0; (iii) boundary-condition loss (type depends on missing BC specification); (iv) Rankine-Hugoniot interface loss penalizing flux-jump mismatches at subdomain boundaries; (v) entropy-residual penalty to suppress expansion shocks. Sampling strategy: use residual-based adaptive refinement (RAR)—after every N epochs, evaluate the weak residual on a fine grid and add collocation points where the residual is large (near shocks and rarefaction edges). Optimization: use Adam for the first 5000 epochs for robust exploration, then switch to L-BFGS for fine convergence. Curriculum: initially train on a smoothed (slightly viscous) version of the problem with a small artificial viscosity ε, then anneal ε → 0 over training to progressively sharpen fronts while maintaining stability. Learning rate scheduling with cosine decay. Hyperparameters: 4–6 subdomain networks, each with 4 hidden layers of 64 neurons, tanh activation; quadrature with ≥ 20 points per patch.

model_answer: 1. Shock speed error: if Rankine-Hugoniot conditions are not tightly enforced, the model may predict shocks propagating at incorrect speeds, leading to large L¹ errors that grow linearly in time. 2. Contact smearing: contact discontinuities carry no pressure jump and are easily over-diffused by the network, resulting in artificially thickened density profiles. 3. Gibbs-type oscillations: if the weak-form test functions are too coarse or subdomain boundaries are misaligned, spurious overshoots in density and pressure may appear near shocks, potentially driving p < 0 and causing solver crash. 4. Entropy violation: the network may converge to a non-physical weak solution (expansion shock) if the entropy penalty weight is too small. 5. Poor generalization: a model trained on one specific Riemann problem may not generalize to different left/right states or wave configurations (e.g., shock-shock vs. shock-rarefaction interactions) without retraining or meta-learning. 6. Validation metrics must include: L¹ and L∞ errors against an exact Riemann solver (e.g., Roe or HLLC), shock-location error, conservation error (total mass/momentum/energy drift), and minimum pressure check. Relying solely on pointwise MSE is insufficient because it masks localized but physically critical errors at discontinuities.

model_answer: The task requires modeling 2D Rayleigh-Bénard convection using the Boussinesq approximation to couple the Navier-Stokes equations (velocity, pressure) with the advection-diffusion equation (temperature). Missing specifications include the exact Rayleigh number (Ra), Prandtl number (Pr), cell aspect ratio, and side-wall boundary conditions. The absence of Ra and Pr prevents determining the flow regime (laminar rolls vs. turbulent) and the thickness of the thermal and kinetic boundary layers. The high-contrast nature of K introduces stiffness: gradients of h concentrate in low-permeability barriers and along high-permeability channels, creating localized features that standard uniform sampling will miss. The fine-scale channels impose a resolution requirement—the model must capture spatial scales much smaller than the domain size, which challenges both collocation-based and operator-learning approaches.

Channel resolution failure: if fine-scale channels are narrower than the effective receptive field or collocation density, the model will smooth over them, producing large local head errors and incorrect flux pathways—mitigated by adaptive sampling and multi-scale architectures. 2. High-contrast stiffness: neural networks exhibit spectral bias toward low-frequency solutions; high-contrast K produces sharp head gradients that are systematically under-resolved, leading to non-physical flux leakage across low-permeability barriers. 3. Mass conservation violation: soft PDE enforcement allows global mass imbalance, which is unacceptable for reservoir or groundwater applications—mitigated by hard flux constraints or Lagrange multiplier augmentation. 4. Out-of-distribution K fields: a surrogate trained on one statistical ensemble (e.g., Gaussian random fields with specific correlation length) may fail on geologically distinct channel patterns; this risk is unspecified in the task and must be addressed by characterizing the K distribution. 5. Boundary condition misspecification: since BC types are not detailed, incorrect BC implementation will corrupt the entire solution; sensitivity analysis on BC parameters is required. 6. Training instability: high-contrast coefficients cause gradient variance explosion in PDE loss terms, requiring gradient clipping or normalized loss formulations.
